title: Classification: Sorting into Categories
concept: Classification is the supervised learning task of sorting data into a fixed set of discrete categories, or classes, such as spam vs. not-spam or cat vs. dog vs. bird.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does a regression model predict?

options.text: A continuous number, like a price or temperature
A category from a fixed list
A hand-written rule

exercises.explain: Right! Regression predicts a number. Today we look at its counterpart: classification.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Regression and classification always solve the exact same kind of problem.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: False! They answer different questions — regression asks "how much?" and classification asks "which one?"

cards.title: Sorting laundry
cards.body: Before doing laundry, you sort clothes into piles: whites, colors, and delicates. You don't invent a new pile for every shirt — you pick from a fixed set of piles. That's exactly what classification does with data.

cards.title: What classification predicts
cards.body: Classification predicts a category (also called a class) from a fixed set of options — not a number, but a label like spam or cat.

cards.title: Mail slots, not a number line
cards.body: Think of classification like sorting letters into labeled mail slots — "Bills", "Personal", "Junk". Every letter goes into exactly one slot. There's no slot for "kind of a bill" — it's one option or another.

cards.title: Two kinds of sorting
cards.body: An inbox sorts each email into spam or not spam — just two options, called binary classification. A photo app sorts each picture into cat, dog, or bird — three or more options, called multiclass classification.

cards.title: It's not always just two choices
cards.body: You might think classification always means picking between two options, like yes or no. In reality, classification can sort data into many categories — a music app might classify a song's genre into ten or more classes, not just two.

exercises.prompt: A streaming app sorts each show into "Comedy", "Drama", "Documentary", or "Horror". What kind of classification is this?

options.text: Multiclass classification
Binary classification
Regression

exercises.explain: Right! Four possible categories means this is multiclass classification.

exercises.prompt: True or false: A model that decides if a transaction is "fraud" or "not fraud" is doing binary classification.

options.text: True
False

exercises.explain: True! Exactly two categories — fraud or not fraud — makes this binary classification.

exercises.sentence: Classification sorts data into a fixed set of categories, also called ___.
exercises.explain: Each category a classification model can choose from is called a class.

exercises.prompt: Which of these outputs comes from a classification model?

options.text: Species: Bird
Estimated weight: 3.2 lbs
Predicted rainfall: 0.4 inches

exercises.explain: "Bird" is a category chosen from a fixed set of species — a classification output.

exercises.prompt: Why is sorting photos into "cat", "dog", or "bird" NOT a regression task?

options.text: Because the output is one of a fixed set of categories, not a number on a scale
Because animals can't be measured
Because there are exactly two choices
Because photos don't have data in them

exercises.explain: Exactly! The output is a category chosen from a fixed list, which is the definition of classification.

exercises.prompt: A hospital lab wants to sort blood samples into "Type A", "Type B", "Type AB", or "Type O". Should this be built as classification, and why?

options.text: Yes, because there's a fixed set of categories and every sample belongs to exactly one
No, because blood type should be measured as a number
No, because there are too many options for classification
Yes, but only because doctors prefer categories

exercises.explain: Right! Four fixed blood-type categories, each sample fitting exactly one, is a textbook classification problem.

exercises.prompt: A ticket support system needs to route each customer message to one of five departments: Billing, Technical, Shipping, Returns, or General. A separate tool estimates how many minutes it will take an agent to resolve each ticket. Which statement is correct?

options.text: Routing to a department is classification; estimating resolve time is regression
Both are classification because both involve support tickets
Both are regression because they both output a result
Routing to a department is regression because there are five departments

exercises.explain: Exactly! Picking one of five fixed departments is classification, while estimating minutes is regression — same system, two task types.
